subject: [PATCH 3/4] iova: limit_pfn-aware augmentation for log(n) 32-bit alloc

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that an anchor node is still needed in the rbtree to keep track of the largest gap, and prefers using it over special casing.

The author acknowledged that walking the rbtree from the root is sufficient to find the correct address range in O(log n) time, and therefore the additional augmented data is not needed.

Author acknowledges a concern about the choice of data structure for pruning in __iova_search_free_gap, agrees that the maple tree might be a better option and offers to send out v3 with this change

Author acknowledged that the iova slab cache's SLAB_HWCACHE_ALIGN setting is causing memory waste, agreed to drop it and test a new version (v3) of the code, which uses a maple tree instead of an augmented rbtree.
reviews.raw_body:
We kind of still need the anchor node, as the highest thing
in the rbtree, so we can use that as bookkeeping for the
largest gap.

Without it, we would not know there was an enormous 64 bit
gap if the first iova allocation is made with a 32 bit
mask.

We could add special casing, but the anchor node might be
simpler and smaller.

---

You are right, we do not need this. Walking the rbtree
from the root can bring us to the right address range
in O(log n) time, without any need for additional
augmented data.

---

I have this implemented here now.

I can send out v3 if you would like to see that version,
but you are right that the maple tree may be better!

---

Doing this allows the struct iova to shrink from
40 bytes down to 16 bytes. However the iova slab
cache seems to specify SLAB_HWCACHE_ALIGN, resulting
in 64 bytes used per iova.

If we drop SLAB_HWCACHE_ALIGN and assume 70-90%
full maple tree nodes (I assume many iova requests
are of same/similar size - let me know if that's
wrong), we are looking at a reduction of memory
use per iova from 64 bytes allocated, to around
44-60 bytes per iova on average.

This is with allocation becoming O(log n).

Maple tree may be the way to go.

Let me go test this code, and I'd be happy
to post this as v3, instead of the latest
augmented rbtree code :)


-- 
All Rights Reversed.

2026-05-29.patch_summary: This patch improves the performance of 32-bit DMA allocation in a mixed-DMA-mask domain by introducing a new augmented field that bounds the search by 32-bit-clamped gap size. The new field, __subtree_max_gap32, is precomputed and stored on each iova whenever the gap_to_prev changes. This allows the augmented callbacks to remain pure functions of the node without needing domain context. As a result, for 32-bit allocations, the search time is reduced from O(n) to O(log n). The patch also updates the compute helper iova_compute_max() to update both __subtree_max_gap and __subtree_max_gap32 in one pass.
